name: Sionna Therapeutics, Inc.
business: Sionna Therapeutics is a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company developing novel medicines that normalize the function of the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein for people with cystic fibrosis (CF). The company's lead product candidates, SION-719 and SION-451, are NBD1 stabilizers designed to correct defects caused by the F508del genetic mutation, which is carried by approximately 90% of people with CF. Sionna is also advancing a portfolio of complementary CFTR modulator candidates, including SION-2222 and SION-109, for use in combination with its NBD1 stabilizers.
sellsTo: cystic fibrosis patients with the F508del mutation

exposures.sentence: We are a clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company on a mission to revolutionize the current treatment paradigm for cystic fibrosis (CF) by developing novel medicines that normalize the function of the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein to deliver clinically meaningful benefit to people with CF.

catalysts.event: Phase 2a proof-of-concept trial (PreciSION CF) topline data for SION-719
catalysts.sentence: Topline data from this trial is anticipated in mid-2026.

catalysts.event: Phase 1 dual combination trial topline data for SION-451 with SION-2222 and SION-109
catalysts.sentence: Topline data from this trial is anticipated in mid-2026.

tamClaims.claim: Worldwide revenue for approved CFTR modulators was approximately $12 billion in 2025, and it is expected to grow to over $15 billion by 2030.
tamClaims.sentence: Worldwide revenue for approved CFTR modulators was approximately $12 billion in 2025, and it is expected to grow to over $15 billion by 2030.

tamClaims.claim: An estimated 106,000 people have been diagnosed with CF across 94 countries.
tamClaims.sentence: An estimated 106,000 people have been diagnosed with CF across 94 countries, which includes approximately 40,000 adults and children living with this disease in the U.S., according to the Cystic Fibrosis Foundation (CFF).
